payload.summary: All five amendments identify genuine defects or robustness gaps in retry, timeout cleanup, and error classification code. Each targets a concrete failure mode — thundering-herd amplification, leaked processes/sessions on teardown failure, and misclassified spawn errors — that can cause real operational problems. Amendments fe174b87 and c240683f overlap (same tmux session-leak bug from different angles) but are complementary: one proposes a fallback in the caller, the other a structural fix in cancel() itself. Both should be accepted.

votes.rationale: Adding jitter to exponential backoff is a well-established best practice. The current deterministic backoff (5s, 10s, 20s, 40s, 60s) means that concurrent panel members hitting the same rate limit will all retry at exactly the same moments, creating thundering-herd amplification. This is especially relevant in this system where multiple panel members run concurrently against shared API backends. ±20-30% uniform jitter is low-risk, easy to implement, and meaningfully improves retry behavior under load.

The code at tmux.rs:540 calls self.cancel() on timeout, but cancel() at line 633-636 calls take_active_session() first, immediately removing the session from the tracking HashMap. If the subsequent SIGTERM/SIGKILL fails, the session is orphaned — not killed, not tracked, and future cancel attempts will no-op because the session is no longer in the map. The amendment's proposed fallback (directly calling kill_session and cleanup on the still-in-scope session Arc) is a practical fix that prevents tmux session leakage without requiring a structural redesign of cancel().

This is a real process-leak and workspace-conflict bug. At lines 1048-1063, the timeout handler fires spawn_background_reap (fire-and-forget async kill) and then immediately removes the child from active_children and returns the Timeout error. At lines 1477-1480, spawn_background_reap drops both force_kill() and wait() errors silently. If kill fails, the hung process keeps running but the adapter has forgotten about it, so the retry loop can spawn a second process against the same workspace while the first is still alive. Synchronous teardown confirmation — or at minimum surfacing the failure — is needed to prevent this.

This is the structural complement to fe174b87, addressing the root cause rather than the symptom. cancel() at line 633-636 removes the session from active_sessions before attempting any cleanup (SIGTERM, SIGKILL, kill-session). This violates the principle that a resource should only be deregistered after teardown is confirmed. If any cleanup step fails, the session is orphaned with no way to re-attempt cleanup. The fix — keeping the session tracked until teardown succeeds, or treating cleanup failure as a transport error — is the correct structural approach and should be implemented alongside or instead of fe174b87's fallback.

The ENOENT classification at lines 996-1005 is genuinely ambiguous. Command::spawn() returns ErrorKind::NotFound not only when the binary is missing, but also when the working directory doesn't exist, or when a script's shebang interpreter is missing. These are distinct failure modes with different appropriate responses: a missing working dir might be a transient setup issue worth retrying, while a missing binary is terminal. Classifying all ENOENT as BinaryNotFound causes non-binary-related failures to skip the expanded retry budget and become terminal prematurely. The fix should verify the binary exists (e.g., which/Path::exists check) before committing to BinaryNotFound.
